preFix.title: Sharpening hand saws
preFix.subtitle: Jointing, setting, and filing a crosscut or rip hand saw
preFix.excerpt: A guide to restoring the cutting edge of a hand saw: jointing the teeth to even height, setting the teeth alternately left and right, and filing each tooth to the correct profile. Covers both crosscut and rip-tooth patterns.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery for saw-tooth geometry, set, and filing sequence. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for hand-saw maintenance and sharpening.

content.text: A hand saw that feels slow and requires more force than it should is usually dull. Restoring it involves three steps in order: jointing (evening the tooth height), setting (bending alternate teeth outward), and filing (resharpening each tooth face). A saw with irregular teeth or heavy corrosion takes longer than a saw that is only dull; the process is the same.
A crosscut saw severs fibres across the grain; each tooth is filed with a

angle of 60 to 75 degrees across the tooth line, creating a knife edge on each face. A rip saw cuts along the grain; the teeth are filed perpendicular to the tooth line with no fleam, like a row of tiny chisels. Most general-purpose hand saws are crosscut or a compromise pattern.

Hold a flat mill file in a wooden block so that it rides flat along the tops of the teeth. Pass the file along the full length of the saw in one direction. After each pass, sight along the tooth line from the toe of the saw. When every tooth shows a small bright flat on its tip, all teeth are at the same height and jointing is complete.
is applied with a saw set tool, which bends alternate teeth to each side by a consistent amount. The teeth bent in one direction should all be set by the same amount; uneven set produces a
that wanders to one side. Set only the teeth that point away from you on the first pass, then flip the saw and set the other teeth.

Clamp the saw blade, teeth up, in a filing vice or between two wooden jaws in a bench vice. Use a slim taper saw file. File every other tooth first (those bent away from you), holding the file at the correct fleam angle for a crosscut saw or perpendicular for a rip saw. Two to three strokes per tooth are usually enough to remove the bright flat left by jointing. Then turn the saw around and file the remaining teeth from the other side.
When to sharpen versus when to replace

A saw that has been sharpened several times will have teeth that are shorter than the original profile and a gullet that is reduced in depth. When the gullet becomes too shallow to clear sawdust efficiently, the teeth need to be re-profiled by filing down into the gullet, which requires more work than routine sharpening. Inexpensive hardpoint saws with hardened teeth cannot be sharpened at all; they are replaced rather than resharpened.
